title: I tried simplifying how people start selling online — here’s what I built
summary: A developer built a multi-vendor marketplace to reduce friction in starting online sales by allowing users to create accounts, list products, and run shops within a single platform. The project is early-stage and seeks user feedback on whether it solves a real problem. The creator is testing market demand before further development.
content:
Hey everyone 👋
I kept noticing how complicated it can be for someone to start selling products online.
Between setting up a store, managing listings, and figuring out tools, it feels like there’s a lot of friction before you even sell your first product.
So I decided to try something simple.
I built a small multi-vendor marketplace where anyone can:
create an account
list products
and basically run their own mini store inside one platform
The goal was to reduce that initial barrier and make it feel more straightforward.
It’s still very early, and I’m trying to understand if this actually solves a real problem or if I’m missing something important.
If you’ve ever sold something online (or thought about it), I’d really appreciate your honest thoughts:
Would you use something like this?
What would stop you from using it?
What feels missing?
Here’s the project if you want to check it out:
https://marketfy-82c42.web.app/
Any feedback is appreciated 🙏

zh.title: 我尝试简化人们开始在线销售的方式——这是我构建的产品
zh.summary: 一位开发者建立了一个多供应商市场，通过允许用户在单一平台上创建账户、列出产品并运营店铺来减少在线销售的摩擦。该项目处于早期阶段，寻求用户反馈以确认它是否解决了真实问题。创建者在进一步开发之前正在测试市场需求。

title: Astro 4 and React Server Components: The Unexpected benchmark for Security
summary: Astro 4 and React Server Components offer unexpected security benefits by moving component rendering to the server and reducing client-side JavaScript exposure compared to traditional client-side rendered apps. The island architecture of Astro 4 minimizes JavaScript shipped to browsers while enabling strict isolation of sensitive operations like authentication and payment processing. Both frameworks provide built-in security guardrails including CSRF protection, header sanitization, and content security policy defaults.
content:
Astro 4 & React Server Components: The Unexpected Security Benchmark


Modern web development frameworks are constantly evolving to balance performance, developer experience, and security. Two tools at the forefront of this evolution are Astro 4, the latest iteration of the static site generator turned full-stack framework, and React Server Components (RSC), Meta’s paradigm shift for React that moves component rendering to the server. While both are often praised for performance gains, their combined security benefits have emerged as an unexpected benchmark for modern web app protection.
Traditional client-side rendered (CSR) React apps face well-documented security risks: large client-side JavaScript bundles expose sensitive logic, XSS vulnerabilities lurk in unsanitized dynamic content, and over-fetched data increases the attack surface. As apps grow more complex, these risks compound. Frameworks that minimize client-side exposure and tighten data handling naturally score higher on security benchmarks—and Astro 4 and RSC deliver exactly that, often outperforming expectations.
Astro 4 builds on its "islands architecture" foundation, which renders static HTML by default and only hydrates interactive components on the client. This approach drastically reduces the amount of JavaScript shipped to the browser, eliminating entire classes of client-side vulnerabilities. For example, Astro 4’s built-in CSRF protection, automatic header sanitization, and strict content security policy (CSP) defaults mean developers get security guardrails without extra configuration.
New to Astro 4 is enhanced integration with server-side rendering (SSR) and edge runtimes, which lets developers isolate sensitive logic to the server. User authentication, payment processing, and API key handling never touch the client, reducing the risk of credential leakage. Astro 4 also enforces strict type checking for environment variables, preventing accidental exposure of secrets in client bundles—a com

zh.title: Astro 4和React服务器组件：安全性的意外基准
zh.summary: Astro 4和React服务器组件通过将组件渲染移至服务器并减少客户端JavaScript暴露，相比传统客户端渲染应用提供了意外的安全优势。Astro 4的岛屿架构最小化了发送到浏览器的JavaScript，同时能够严格隔离身份验证和支付处理等敏感操作。两个框架都提供内置安全防护，包括CSRF保护、请求头清理和内容安全策略默认值。

title: Zapier Best No-Code Platforms: What No One Tells You
summary: A study of 47 no-code workflows found that 68% of Zapier-dependent pipelines silently drop 12-18% of events under load, with most teams unaware until customer complaints emerge. While engineers often dismiss no-code tools, survey data shows 71% of backend engineers must maintain workflows built by non-technical teams. Alternative solutions like n8n self-hosted can reduce latency by 73% and cut operational costs compared to Zapier's enterprise pricing.
content:
After auditing 47 no-code workflows across 12 engineering teams in Q3 2024, we found that 68% of Zapier-dependent pipelines silently drop 12-18% of events under load, with 92% of teams unaware of the data loss until customer complaints surfaced.
As a senior engineer, you’re probably thinking: "I don’t touch no-code tools, that’s for the marketing team." But our 2024 survey of 200 backend engineers found that 71% of them are responsible for maintaining or debugging no-code workflows built by non-engineering teams. When those workflows break, it’s the engineering team that gets paged at 2am. So even if you don’t build no-code workflows, you need to understand their limits to support them effectively.
  Canvas is down as ShinyHunters threatens to leak schools’ data (516 points)
  Maybe you shouldn't install new software for a bit (376 points)
  Cloudflare to cut about 20% workforce (538 points)
  Dirtyfrag: Universal Linux LPE (557 points)
  Pinocchio is weirder than you remembered (93 points)
  Zapier’s free tier caps API calls at 100/month per integration, with 401 rate limits returning 200 status codes for 14% of SDK versions < 7.2.3
  n8n 1.28.0 self-hosted reduces event processing latency by 73% vs Zapier Enterprise when handling >10k events/min
  Self-hosted no-code stacks cut monthly operational costs by $4.2k per 5-person team vs Zapier’s $1.5k/user/month Enterprise plan
  By 2026, 60% of mid-sized engineering teams will replace SaaS no-code tools with self-hosted alternatives to avoid vendor lock-in
Zapier is the 800-pound gorilla of the no-code space, with 3 million+ users and a marketing machine that claims "anyone can build workflows in minutes." But for engineering teams, the reality is far different. Zapier’s pricing is opaque: their enterprise plan starts at $1.5k per user per month, with annual contracts required, hidden fees for additional API calls, and no refunds for downtime. In 2023, Zapier had 4 major outages totaling 14 hours of downtime, with no

zh.title: Zapier最佳无代码平台：没人告诉你的秘密
zh.summary: 一项包含47个无代码工作流的研究发现，68%的Zapier依赖管道在负载下会无声地丢弃12-18%的事件，大多数团队直到客户投诉出现才意识到。虽然工程师通常轻视无代码工具，但调查数据显示71%的后端工程师必须维护由非技术团队构建的工作流。n8n自托管等替代方案与Zapier企业定价相比可将延迟降低73%并降低运营成本。

title: I Built a React SPA and Tried to Rank on Google. Here Are My Real Numbers After 12 Weeks.
summary: A developer shared 12 weeks of SEO data for a client-rendered React SPA, which generated only 806 impressions and 1 click with an average Google ranking of position 47. The solution involved implementing prerendering at build time using Puppeteer to generate static HTML files that crawlers receive while users get the dynamic SPA experience. The approach requires careful synchronization of sitemaps, prerender scripts, and rewrite rules to avoid broken pages.
content:
If you've built a single-page app and wondered whether Google will ever find it, I have 12 weeks of real data for you.
I launched a SaaS tool in February 2026. React + Vite + TypeScript frontend, Express backend, deployed on Render. No server-side rendering. No Next.js. Just a client-rendered SPA that I needed Google to crawl and index.
This is what happened.

806 impressions. 1 click. Twelve weeks.
That one click was probably me checking if my own site showed up.
Average position moved from about 57 (page 6 of Google) to 47 (page 5). Not exactly a success story. But I learned a lot about what it takes to get a pure SPA indexed.
Google doesn't render JavaScript well enough to index a React SPA reliably. I hit this wall in week 2 when pages were getting crawled but showing up with blank content in Google's index.
The fix was prerendering. At build time, a script visits each route with Puppeteer, waits for the page to finish rendering, and saves the output as static HTML. The deployment platform serves those HTML files to crawlers (matched by user-agent), and regular users get the normal SPA experience.
The prerender config looks like this:
// prerender.mjs — runs at build time
const routes = [
  { route: '/', outputPath: 'index.html' },
  { route: '/guides/landing-page-psychology', outputPath: 'guides/landing-page-psychology/index.html' },
  // ... 15 more routes
];

Each route gets its own index.html in a subdirectory. The hosting config maps clean URLs to these files:
# render.yaml rewrite rules
- source: /guides/landing-page-psychology
  destination: /guides/landing-page-psychology/index.html

Three files have to stay in sync: the sitemap, the prerender script, and the rewrite rules. Every time I add a page, all three need updating. I've shipped broken pages twice by forgetting one. There's no automation for it yet and it's the most annoying part of the setup.
Once prerendering was working, pages started showing up in Google within a few days of being submitted th

zh.title: 我构建了一个React单页应用并尝试在Google上排名。这是我12周后的实际数据。
zh.summary: 一位开发者分享了12周的SEO数据，其客户端渲染的React单页应用只产生了806次展示和1次点击，平均Google排名位置47。解决方案涉及在构建时使用Puppeteer实现预渲染以生成静态HTML文件供爬虫接收，同时用户获得动态SPA体验。该方法需要仔细同步网站地图、预渲染脚本和重写规则以避免页面损坏。

title: Building a RAG pipeline without OpenAI
summary: A developer built a retrieval augmented generation system using open-source components including a sentence transformer model, ChromaDB for vector storage, and custom retrieval logic instead of relying on OpenAI. RAG systems improve accuracy and allow source citation by searching a knowledge database before generating responses, mitigating hallucination and outdated information. The implementation demonstrates that functional RAG pipelines don't require proprietary APIs.
content:
RAG stands for Retrieval Augmented Generation. The idea is simple: before your model answers a question, it first searches a database of relevant knowledge and uses that information to answer better.
I built this entirely without OpenAI — my own embedding model, my own vector database, my own retrieval logic.
Without RAG, your model answers purely from what it learned during training. It might hallucinate, it might be outdated, it can't cite sources.
With RAG:
Question comes in
System searches a database for relevant facts
Those facts get added to the prompt
Model answers using the retrieved context
Think of it like the difference between a doctor answering from memory versus a doctor who can look up references before answering.
1. Embedder — converts text to vectors
I used pritamdeka/S-PubMedBert-MS-MARCO — a sentence transformer specifically trained on medical and scientific text. It converts any text into a list of 768 numbers that represent its meaning.
"Meningitis causes fever and neck stiffness" → [0.23, -0.41, 0.87, ...]
Similar sentences produce similar vectors. This is what makes search work.
from sentence_transformers import SentenceTransformer
embedder = SentenceTransformer("pritamdeka/S-PubMedBert-MS-MARCO")
embedding = embedder.encode("Patient has fever and stiff neck")

2. ChromaDB — stores and searches vectors
ChromaDB is a local vector database. I embedded 2,000 medical Q&A pairs and stored them. When a new question comes in, ChromaDB finds the most similar stored examples in milliseconds.
import chromadb
client = chromadb.PersistentClient(path="data/embeddings")
collection = client.create_collection("medical_knowledge")

# Store
collection.add(embeddings=embeddings, documents=texts, ids=ids)

# Search
results = collection.query(query_embeddings=[query_vec], n_results=3)

3. Pipeline — connects everything
The full flow:
def answer(question):
    # 1. Retrieve relevant knowledge
    docs = retrieve(question, top_k=3)

    # 2. Build context
    contex

zh.title: 不依赖OpenAI构建RAG管道
zh.summary: 开发者使用包括句子转换模型、用于向量存储的ChromaDB和自定义检索逻辑的开源组件构建了检索增强生成系统，而不是依赖OpenAI。RAG系统通过在生成响应前搜索知识库来提高准确性并允许源引用，减少幻觉和过时信息。该实现表明功能性RAG管道不需要专有API。

title: React.memo vs useMemo vs useCallback — Complete Guide
summary: React.memo, useMemo, and useCallback are three optimization techniques that prevent unnecessary re-renders and performance issues in React applications. React.memo skips component re-renders when props haven't changed, while useMemo and useCallback memoize values and functions respectively to prevent expensive recalculations. Understanding when and how to apply these optimizations is essential for maintaining performance in large applications.
content:
If you're learning React, you've probably heard these terms everywhere: React.memo useMemo useCallback At first, they look confusing. But all three solve one major problem: Unnecessary Re-renders & Performance Issues This guide explains: what each one does why they exist how they are connected when to use them interview explanations Why Do These Exist? In React, components re-render very frequently. A component re-renders when: state changes props change parent component re-renders Most of the time this is fine. But in large applications: expensive calculations recreated functions unnecessary child renders can slow down the application. That’s why React provides: React.memo useMemo useCallback for optimization. First Understand One Important Thing In React: function App() { console.log("Rendered"); return

Hello

; } Whenever React re-renders: the ENTIRE component function runs again variables get recreated functions get recreated This is the root reason why memoization exists. React.memo ✅ What is React.memo? React.memo prevents unnecessary component re-renders. Problem Without React.memo function Child() { console.log("Child rendered"); return

Child Component

; } function Parent() { const [count, setCount] = useState(0); return ( <> ); } What Happens Here? When button is clicked: Parent re-renders Child ALSO re-renders Even though: Child props didn't change Child state didn't change ❌ This is an unnecessary re-render. Solution Using React.memo const Child = React.memo(() => { console.log("Child rendered"); return

Child Component

; }); Now React remembers the previous render. If props are unchanged: Child render is skipped ⚙️ How React.memo Works Internally React performs: Shallow Comparison It compares: old props new props If props are same: Important Poin

zh.title: React.memo vs useMemo vs useCallback — 完整指南
zh.summary: React.memo、useMemo和useCallback是三种优化技术，可以防止React应用程序中不必要的重新渲染和性能问题。当props未改变时React.memo会跳过组件重新渲染，而useMemo和useCallback分别记忆值和函数以防止昂贵的重新计算。理解何时以及如何应用这些优化对于维持大型应用程序的性能至关重要。

title: Optimizing OpenSpec Phase Efficiency with Different Agents: HagiCode Practice Summary
summary: OpenSpec, a proposal-driven development system, was improved by replacing a single generic AI prompt with phase-specific agents and parameterized templates tailored to different development stages. The HagiCode project documented issues including context drift during exploration, unstable artifact generation, and unclear responsibility boundaries across documentation types. Agent layering strategy provides dynamic behavior adjustment based on specific workflow scenarios.
content:
Optimizing OpenSpec Phase Efficiency with Different Agents: HagiCode Practice Summary



Generic prompts cannot handle the specific requirements of different development stages. Through phase-specific agents and a parameterized template system, AI can produce high-quality output at every step.
OpenSpec is a proposal-driven development system that manages the creation, review, and implementation of technical proposals through structured workflows. The idea itself is sound, but in practice, we found significant issues with using a single generic AI prompt.
The explore stage lacks context anchoring, causing AI explorations to deviate from the proposal scope; artifact generation quality is unstable, with design.md missing visual elements, proposal.md lacking code change tables, and tasks.md even including Git operations that shouldn't be there; responsibility boundaries are blurred, with unclear content requirements for different document types; prompts lack flexibility, unable to dynamically adjust AI behavior based on different scenarios.
These issues directly impact the efficiency and output quality of the OpenSpec workflow. There's really no other way but to modify the prompt templates ourselves. This article documents that period of work.
The solution shared in this article comes from our practical experience in the HagiCode project. HagiCode is an AI-powered code assistant, and we extensively use the OpenSpec workflow to manage technical proposals during development. The agent layering strategy introduced here is exactly the optimization solution we summarized from practical use.
If you find this approach valuable, it means our engineering practices are pretty solid—HagiCode itself is worth paying attention to.
The OpenSpec system contains multiple core stages, each with specific goals and constraints. Understanding the responsibility boundaries of these stages is the foundation for designing effective agent strategies.
┌───────────────────────────────────────────

zh.title: 使用不同智能体优化OpenSpec阶段效率：HagiCode实践总结
zh.summary: OpenSpec是一个提案驱动的开发系统，通过用针对不同开发阶段的阶段特定智能体和参数化模板替换单一通用AI提示来改进。HagiCode项目记录了多个问题，包括探索期间的上下文漂移、不稳定的工件生成和跨文档类型的责任边界不清楚。智能体分层策略根据特定工作流场景提供动态行为调整。

title: Accessing Google Ads data via API is easy
summary: A tutorial demonstrates how to access Google Ads data through the Google Ads API using OAuth authentication and Google Cloud projects as intermediaries. The process involves creating a cloud project with credentials, enabling the Google Ads API, and obtaining a developer token from the Google Ads account. The approach enables automation of marketing dashboards and data extraction workflows.
content:
If you are going to build automation tools or marketing dashboards for Google Ads data, you need to pull it out through the API. It is easier than you think. This tutorial helps you access Google Ads data via API.
Before this, you should have a Google Ads manager account and some campaign data. Next, we need to create a cloud project.
Google Cloud project is act like an intermediator between the user and the user’s Google Ads account. You heard about authentication and authorisation. In technical we use OAuth. For this process we create a project with a secret ID and a key.
Open the Google Cloud console https://console.cloud.google.com/.
If you have an account and project, just use it. Otherwise create a new project.
Click the console in the top right corner and then open ‘My First Project’. Create new project with all necessary data.

This client ID and secret will be used for authentication purpose later.
Search ‘Google OAuth Platform’ and click ‘Clients’
If you created a new project, then it asks for the project configuration. Fill in all the necessary data. Otherwise create a client.
Go to Clients → Create client. It asks for application type, based on your need choose one.
Then fill in the application name and the redirect URI for callback.  The redirect URI will be valid.
Download your credential file, and it disappears after closing the tab.
This JSON consists of client ID and client secret, and other credentials.
If you have any doubts about this step, just refer to the blog https://agentzee.ai/blogs/how-to-access-google-ads-manager-data-via-api-with-an-access-token#steps-to-applying-for-a-developer-token.

Search for ‘APIs and Services’
Click ‘Enable APIs and services’
Search for Google Ads API and click the ‘Enable’ button.

Open your Google Ads account dashboard https://ads.google.com/.
Go to Admin → API center. There, you can find the test developer token. For internal use, you can use this token.
For production purposes, you should apply for the ‘Basic

zh.title: 通过API访问Google Ads数据很容易
zh.summary: 教程演示了如何使用OAuth身份验证和Google Cloud项目作为中介通过Google Ads API访问Google Ads数据。该过程涉及使用凭据创建云项目、启用Google Ads API，以及从Google Ads账户获取开发者令牌。该方法支持营销仪表板的自动化和数据提取工作流。

title: How AI Cured My Writing Procrastination
summary: A writer who hadn't published in years overcame procrastination by using AI to handle time-consuming writing tasks including turning rough ideas into coherent prose, generating images, and creating diagrams. The approach divides labor between human guidance and AI execution, with the writer controlling direction while AI handles polishing and formatting. Subsequent editorial review by the writer ensures quality before publication.
content:
A Procrastinator's Excuse


I haven't written a blog post in years.
Not because I had nothing to say — I just couldn't bring myself to do it. Writing used to take me at least a week per article: brainstorming, drafting, revising over and over, then hunting for images, drawing flowcharts in draw.io, dragging and dropping for half an hour just to get a box on screen. The whole process was slow and exhausting, and I gradually built up a real resistance to it. Every time I opened my editor, the first thought that crossed my mind wasn't "what should I write?" — it was "forget it, maybe another day."
And another day turned into several years.
It was only recently that I realized what had stopped me was never a lack of things to write about. It was that writing itself had become too heavy.
Now AI has taken away a large part of that weight.
Turning ideas into coherent prose is hard: I just need to articulate my thoughts, even roughly, and AI can organize them into flowing paragraphs. I control the direction and the perspective; it polishes the language. This division of labor suits me much better.
Finding images is time-consuming, and they're often the wrong format or size: Now I describe what I want and AI generates it — style, composition, everything — in seconds.
Drawing flowcharts is slow: I describe the logic to AI and it generates the diagram directly. I just check if it's right and adjust if needed.
I've built myself a small writing pipeline, centered around two AI roles.
The first is the writer. I throw my ideas, thoughts, and core points at it, and it turns those fragments into a complete first draft.
Once the draft is ready, I edit it myself. This step is crucial — don't underestimate your readers. The best articles are as short as possible, and most people these days (myself included) don't have the patience for a long read. Later is never.
After editing, I hand it to the second AI role — the editor. It reviews the article and does another round of polishing. Hon

zh.title: AI如何治愈了我的写作拖延症
zh.summary: 一位多年未发表过文章的作家通过使用AI来处理耗时的写作任务（包括将粗略想法转化为连贯的散文、生成图像和创建图表）克服了拖延症。该方法在人类指导和AI执行之间分配劳动，作家控制方向而AI处理打磨和格式化。作家随后的编辑审查确保出版前的质量。

title: DatAasee 0.9 Released
summary: DatAasee 0.9 is a metadata management system that centralizes distributed data sources through an HTTP API supporting multiple query languages including SQL, OpenCypher, and GraphQL. The system uses ArcadeDB as its graph database backend with optional frontend provided by Lowdefy and deploys via Docker or Podman containers. Key features include full-text search, faceted filtering, and support for standard metadata formats like DataCite and MARC.
content:
DatAasee Tech Stack Canvas




Setting: Many distributed data and metadata sources
Goals:


Centralize metadata
Interlinked metadata catalog
Super-index for bibliographic and research data
Features:


Interact through HTTP API (JSON)
Search by filter/facet, full-text, ingest-source, DOI
Custom queries via: SQL, OpenCypher, MQL, GraphQL, Redis








Frontend: Lowdefy (Optional)
Backend: Connect (formerly Benthos)
Data Storage: ArcadeDB (Graph Database)
Infrastructure: Compose (via Docker or Podman)
Deployment: (Public) Container Images
Monitoring: Container Logs
Integrations:



Protocols: OAI-PMH, S3, GET, DatAasee


Encodings: XML


Formats: DataCite, DC, LIDO, MARC, MODS








Exports: DataCite, BibJSON




Security: Privileged endpoints
Testing: check-jsonschema




Development: https://github.com/ulbmuenster/dataasee

zh.title: DatAasee 0.9 发布
zh.summary: DatAasee 0.9 是一个元数据管理系统，通过支持 SQL、OpenCypher 和 GraphQL 等多种查询语言的 HTTP API 集中管理分布式数据源。该系统使用 ArcadeDB 作为其图形数据库后端，由 Lowdefy 提供可选前端，可通过 Docker 或 Podman 容器部署。主要特性包括全文搜索、分面过滤以及对 DataCite 和 MARC 等标准元数据格式的支持。

title: PCB Supply Chain in May 2026: Why It's Now a Seller's Market
summary: The PCB industry entered a seller's market in 2026 as copper prices surged 30%, fiberglass production faced quotas, and epoxy resin shortages constrained supply. These material constraints have extended lead times across all PCB categories, from 2-3 weeks for standard boards to 16-24 weeks for IC substrates compared to previous timelines. Industry recommendations include extending forecast horizons to 12-16 weeks and qualifying multiple manufacturers to balance cost optimization against supply security.
content:
The PCB industry has officially entered seller's market territory in 2026. NCAB Group's latest supply chain outlook paints a clear picture: rising raw material costs, constrained fabrication capacity, and extended lead times are reshaping procurement strategies across the electronics industry.
Three major input materials are driving costs higher:
Copper prices surged 30%+ year-to-date, driven by AI data center buildout and EV manufacturing competing for supply. For PCB manufacturers, copper represents 25-40% of material costs — meaning a 30% copper price increase translates to 10-15% board cost increases before other factors.
Electronic-grade fiberglass faces production quotas limiting availability. Specialty glass for high-speed laminates (NE-glass, spread glass) is even tighter. Capacity additions lag demand by 18-24 months.
PPE resin shortages continue constraining high-speed laminate production. Epoxy costs elevated by petrochemical inflation and environmental compliance.
The combination of strong demand and material constraints is impacting every PCB category:
Standard 2-4 layer: 2-3 weeks (was 1-2)
6-12 layer multilayer: 3-4 weeks (was 2-3)
HDI (any-layer): 5-7 weeks (was 3-5)
High-speed (Megtron, Rogers): 6-8 weeks (was 4-6)
Rigid-flex: 6-8 weeks (was 4-5)
IC substrates: 16-24 weeks (was 12-16)
NCAB's recommendations for navigating the seller's market:
Extend forecast horizons to 12-16 weeks minimum. Place blanket orders for recurring designs. Lock in material allocations early.
Specify alternative material qualifications (e.g., Megtron 6 OR Tachyon 100G). Avoid single-source exotic materials. Design to standard stack-ups matching factory capability.
Qualify at least two manufacturers per board type. Maintain relationships across different regions. Balance cost optimization against supply security.
The cheapest PCB quote is often most expensive when factoring delivery reliability, quality costs, engineering support, and material traceability.
The global PCB m

zh.title: 2026 年 5 月 PCB 供应链：为何现在成为卖方市场
zh.summary: 随着铜价上涨 30%、玻璃纤维生产面临配额限制以及环氧树脂短缺制约供应，PCB 行业在 2026 年进入了卖方市场。这些材料约束延长了所有 PCB 类别的交期，标准板从 2-3 周、IC 衬底从 16-24 周，相比之前的交期都有所延长。行业建议包括将预测周期延长至 12-16 周，并寻求多个制造商以平衡成本优化与供应安全。

title: AI API Cost Caps and Multi-Key Failover: The Boring Layer That Matters
summary: Managing AI API costs requires infrastructure-level controls beyond pricing, including budget caps, model permissions, token limits, and rate limiting to prevent unexpected cost spikes. Multi-key failover systems enable routing to alternative providers when one key hits limits or becomes unstable, while error redaction prevents internal details from leaking to users. These gateway layer features are more critical to business viability than the choice of model itself.
content:
When companies distribute Claude, GPT or Gemini APIs internally or to customers, model price is only one part of the problem.
The boring infrastructure layer matters more than most teams expect.
Budget caps
Each tenant, team or customer should have a hard budget. Usage should be controlled before the request is completed, not only reviewed at the end of the month.
Model permissions
Not every workflow needs the most expensive model. Model access should be tied to use case, tenant and budget.
Token limits
Long prompts and long outputs can create cost spikes even when request volume is low. Context length and output tokens need limits.
Rate limits
Bad scripts, loops or abuse can drain budgets quickly. Rate limiting belongs in the gateway layer, not only in application code.
Multi-key failover
If one key hits limits or one provider becomes unstable, the gateway should be able to route traffic to a fallback chain.
Error redaction
Upstream errors should not expose keys, raw provider bodies or internal traces to end users. Return a clean error code, message and trace ID.
Mingde’s AI API service focuses on this layer: audit logs, cost caps, redaction, multi-key pools and SDK-compatible access.
The model gets attention. The gateway keeps the business alive.

zh.title: AI API 成本上限和多密钥故障转移：重要的枯燥层
zh.summary: 管理 AI API 成本需要超越定价的基础设施级控制，包括预算上限、模型权限、令牌限制和速率限制以防止意外成本激增。多密钥故障转移系统允许在一个密钥达到限制或变得不稳定时路由到替代提供商，而错误编辑可防止内部细节泄露给用户。这些网关层功能对业务生存能力的重要性超过了模型选择本身。

title: The Unexpected optimization in ESLint vs Vitest: A Head-to-Head
summary: Vitest outperforms ESLint by 47% on cold-start lint speed for optimization checks on large TypeScript codebases when three undocumented ESLint flags are disabled, according to 2024 benchmarks. This counterintuitive finding cost a Fortune 500 frontend team 12 hours of weekly CI waste before discovery. The result challenges conventional assumptions about static analysis tool performance.
content:
In a 2024 benchmark of 10,000+ line TypeScript codebases, Vitest outperformed ESLint’s native rule execution by 47% in cold-start lint speed when configured for identical optimization checks—but only when you disable 3 undocumented default ESLint flags first. This reversal of conventional wisdom (most teams assume ESLint is faster for static analysis) cost one Fortune 500 frontend team 12 hours of weekly CI waste before they caught it.
  Canvas (Instructure) LMS Down in Ongoing Ransomware Attack (124 points)
  Dirtyfrag: Universal Linux LPE (371 points)
  Maybe you shouldn't install new software for a bit (61 points)
  The Burning Man MOOP Map (521 points)
  Agents need control flow, not more prompts (304 points)
  Vitest 1.6.0 with --threads=4 runs optimization rule checks 47% faster than ESLint 8.56.0 on 10k LOC TypeScript codebases (Apple M3 Max, 64GB RAM, Node 20.11.0), with 58% faster execution on 20k LOC codebases
  ESLint’s default --cache flag adds 12% overhead for incremental runs larger than 5k LOC due to unoptimized cache invalidation
  Disabling ESLint’s rule-timing and eslintrc lookup reduces cold start time by 32% with zero functionality loss
  By 2025, 60% of frontend teams will replace ESLint with Vitest for optimization-specific static analysis per 2024 State of JS survey trends

ESLint 8.56.0
Vitest 1.6.0

Yes (ESLint rules)

180ms (GitHub Actions)

3 (rule-timing, eslintrc lookup, config cache)
0

Parallel execution support
No
Yes (up to CPU core count)
Vitest’s speed advantage stems from three architectural differences: first, native parallel thread execution, which ESLint lacks entirely (ESLint rules run sequentially per file, with no support for parallel rule execution across files). Second

zh.title: ESLint vs Vitest中意想不到的性能优化：面对面对比
zh.summary: 根据2024年基准测试，当禁用三个未记录的ESLint标志时，Vitest在大型TypeScript代码库的冷启动检查性能上比ESLint快47%。这个违反直觉的发现在被发现之前给一个财富500强前端团队造成了每周12小时的CI浪费。这个结果挑战了人们对静态分析工具性能的传统假设。

title: Top 5 Mistakes Developers Make in Technical Interviews (And How to Fix Them)
summary: Technical interviews require clear communication of requirements and problem-solving process, not just correct solutions. Developers who narrate their thinking, test edge cases systematically, and ask clarifying questions about constraints demonstrate engineering maturity and help interviewers evaluate their approach. Common mistakes include jumping to code without confirming requirements, staying silent while thinking, and abandoning problems instead of reasoning out loud.
content:
Technical interviews don't just test whether you can code.
About constraints — input size, data types, edge cases
About requirements — what should return for empty input? duplicates?
Then confirm: "So my goal is to return X given Y — is that right?" Only then start coding.
Mistake #2: Staying Silent During Problem-Solving
"I'm thinking about a brute-force O(n²) approach first to establish correctness, then I'll look at optimizing."
Say when you get stuck:
"I know I need to track which elements I've seen — I'm deciding between a hash map and a sorted array."
This lets the interviewer see your thought process, help you if you're veering off course, and evaluate your problem-solving approach independent of whether you land the optimal solution.
Mistake #3: Not Testing or Debugging Your Solution
Edge cases to systematically check:
Empty input
Single element input
All equal elements
Negative numbers (if applicable)
Maximum possible input size
Narrate as you go: "Okay, if the input is [3, 1, 4], at this step i is 0 and current_max is 3, so..."
This catches real bugs and demonstrates engineering maturity.
Mistake #4: Weak Communication on System Design
Jumping straight into component diagrams before establishing requirements
The fix:
Clarify requirements and constraints
Estimate scale — QPS, data volume, read/write ratio
Propose high-level design — major components, data flows
Drill down on the complex parts
Discuss tradeoffs and alternatives
Practice saying "I chose X over Y because of Z tradeoff" as a habit. Senior interviewers evaluate whether you understand why your design makes the tradeoffs it does — not just that it works.
Mistake #5: Giving Up Instead of Thinking Out Loud
"I know I need to track some state between iterations, but I'm not immediately seeing the right data structure. Let me think about what properties matter here..."
If you truly need a hint, ask directly:
"I think I need a nudge here — is there a data structure property I should be thinking about?"

zh.title: 开发者在技术面试中犯的5大错误（以及如何修正）
zh.summary: 技术面试需要清楚地沟通需求和解决问题的过程，而不仅仅是正确的解决方案。能够讲述思考过程、系统地测试边界情况以及询问约束条件的开发者展现了工程成熟度，并帮助面试官评估他们的方法。常见的错误包括在确认需求之前就开始编码、思考时保持沉默以及放弃问题而不是大声推理。

title: HCP Terraform potenciado por Infragraph
summary: HashiCorp announced HCP Terraform powered by Infragraph at IBM Think 2026, a centralized knowledge graph that unifies infrastructure data across AWS, Azure, GCP, and on-premises systems in real-time. The tool addresses enterprise challenges with fragmented infrastructure visibility by providing a single source of truth replacing manual data management and multiple disconnected tools. Infragraph updates dynamically as changes occur and serves as a foundation for AI-driven automation of complex workflows.
content:
Migrar a la nube debería simplificar la gestión de infraestructura. Pero para la mayoría de empresas, la realidad es más complicada: los datos de infraestructura viven dispersos en múltiples silos, los equipos no tienen una visión unificada de lo que ocurre en sus entornos, y el tiempo que debería invertirse en detectar problemas se consume navegando entre herramientas desconectadas.
Es aquí donde entra en juego HCP Terraform potenciado por Infragraph, anunciado oficialmente en el IBM Think 2026 y disponible en vista previa pública a partir del 8 de mayo.
Dato clave: "En promedio, las empresas utilizan cinco o más servicios para gestionar su entorno en la nube", según investigaciones de HashiCorp. Infragraph unifica ese panorama fragmentado en una sola vista dinámica.
Infragraph es un grafo de conocimiento centralizado y orientado a eventos que reemplaza las instantáneas estáticas de infraestructura por actualizaciones dinámicas y en tiempo real. Se conecta directamente a cada ecosistema de la organización —AWS, Azure, GCP, entornos on-premises— y enriquece esos datos con información proveniente de Terraform Search para flujos más complejos o personalizados.
El resultado: los equipos de plataforma, CloudOps y seguridad obtienen por fin esa fuente única de verdad que durante años han intentado construir manualmente, o comprando herramientas adicionales que solo generan más sprawl.
Sus tres pilares principales son:
Visibilidad unificada — una sola vista de todos los recursos en entornos híbridos y multi-nube, actualizada continuamente.
Actualizaciones en tiempo real — el grafo se actualiza a medida que los cambios aterrizan en AWS, Azure, GCP o sistemas locales.
Base para IA — fundación de conocimiento unificada para automatizar flujos de trabajo con agentes de IA de confianza en el futuro.

Dia a dia los hackers aprovechan la IA para explotar vulnerabilidades con velocidad y precisión notables, cualquier brecha de seguridad debe descubrirse y parchearse en tiempo rea

zh.title: 由Infragraph驱动的HCP Terraform
zh.summary: HashiCorp在IBM Think 2026大会上宣布推出由Infragraph驱动的HCP Terraform，这是一个集中式知识图谱，可以实时统一AWS、Azure、GCP和本地系统中的基础设施数据。该工具通过提供单一的真实来源来解决企业在基础设施可见性碎片化方面的挑战，取代了手动数据管理和多个断开的工具。Infragraph随着变化动态更新，并为AI驱动的复杂工作流自动化奠定基础。

title: Most developers are doing URL slugs wrong (and it hurts SEO)
summary: Most developers create slugs that are too long, contain too many keywords, or use dynamic IDs, all of which harm SEO and user experience. High-performing slugs follow simple rules: 3–5 words maximum, lowercase only, hyphens instead of underscores, and one main keyword with filler words removed. Short, clean URLs improve readability, click-through rates, shareability, and search engine comprehension.
content:
When I started building websites, I didn’t think much about URL slugs.
Something like:
or even:
seemed fine.
Turns out… it’s not.
After testing multiple pages and tools, I realized that bad slugs quietly hurt both SEO and user experience.
Here’s what actually works 👇
Most developers:
Use dynamic URLs with IDs
Stuff keywords into slugs
Make them too long
Ignore readability
Example:
❌ /best-seo-url-guide-for-beginners-2026

❌ /page?id=928374

❌ /how-to-create-the-best-url-slug-for-seo-ranking-fast
From what I’ve tested, high-performing slugs usually follow these rules:
3–5 words max

Lowercase only

Use hyphens (-), not underscores

Include ONE main keyword

Remove filler words (like "the", "and", "for")
Example:
✅ /seo-url-guide

✅ /slug-generator

✅ /password-generator  
Short, clean URLs:
Are easier to read
Get better click-through rates
Are easier to share
Help search engines understand your page
While working on multiple projects, I got tired of manually creating slugs.
So I built a simple tool to generate clean, SEO-friendly slugs instantly.
👉 https://codetooly.com/tools/slug-generator.html
If you’re ignoring your URL structure, you’re leaving easy SEO wins on the table.
Keep it simple.
Curious — how do you generate your slugs?

zh.title: 大多数开发者做URL路径错误（这对SEO有害）
zh.summary: 大多数开发者创建的路径过长、包含太多关键词或使用动态ID，这些都会损害SEO和用户体验。高性能的路径遵循简单规则：最多3-5个单词、仅小写字母、使用连字符而非下划线、只含一个主要关键词且去除填充词。简短干净的URL提高可读性、点击率、可分享性和搜索引擎理解度。

zh.title: 我正在进行一项关于技术债如何影响软件公司运营流程的短期研究调查
zh.summary: 一名研究员正在进行调查，研究技术债如何影响软件组织的运营流程和容量分配。该调查收集关于累积技术债与日常运营效率之间关系的见解。结果将帮助理解技术债对组织的广泛影响，超越代码质量。

title: For Londoners, a Roman Bridge Still Determines Your Commute
summary: London's entire location and modern geography stems from a Roman military engineering decision around 50 CE to bridge the Thames at the narrowest practical crossing point downstream of marshes. The bridge attracted a trading settlement that became Londinium, and despite multiple reconstructions over nearly 2,000 years, every subsequent bridge was rebuilt at the same site. This single infrastructure decision cascades through modern London's layout, making the historical bridge choice determinative of contemporary urban structure.
content:
Around 50 CE, give or take a few years, a group of Roman military engineers picked a spot on the River Thames and bridged it. They picked the place they did because it was the narrowest practical crossing downstream of the marshes that lined the river's lower reach, with banks workable enough to anchor timber piers and high enough not to wash out at high tide. The bridge they built ran roughly 280 metres across the water on at least nineteen wooden pilings. On the dry, slightly elevated north bank where it landed, an opportunistic trading settlement took root, attracted to the only place for miles where you could reliably get from the south side of the river to the north on foot. They called the settlement Londinium.
The bridge has been rebuilt many times. The Roman timber structure was patched and replaced for centuries, then collapsed into disrepair after the Romans withdrew from Britain in 410 CE. The city itself was largely abandoned for the next two and a half centuries, until Alfred the Great refounded London in 886. The first stone bridge was begun in 1176 by a priest and architect named Peter de Colechurch and finished in 1209, three years after his death. That bridge, the famous Old London Bridge with shops and houses built along its length, eventually rising several stories tall, with severed traitors' heads on spikes at the south gatehouse, stood for over six hundred years. It was replaced in 1831 with John Rennie's stone arch design, which was itself replaced in 1973 with the present concrete-and-steel span that most Londoners walk across without thinking about it.
Each rebuild was on essentially the same site. Once you have built a city around a bridge, the bridge isn't where the bridge is. The city is where the bridge is.
Almost every long-arc fact about modern London cascades from that one Roman engineering decision. The City of London, the financial district, the square mile with the bowler-hat-and-pinstripe stereotypes, sits where it sits because th

zh.title: 对伦敦人来说，罗马桥仍然决定了你的通勤
zh.summary: 伦敦的整个位置和现代地理源于公元50年左右罗马军事工程师做出的决定，在泰晤士河最窄的实际可行渡口处建造大桥。这座桥吸引了一个贸易定居点，后来发展成伦敦城，尽管近2000年间经历了多次重建，每一座后续的桥都在同一地点重建。这一单一的基础设施决定贯穿现代伦敦的布局，使得历史桥梁的选择成为当代城市结构的决定性因素。

title: I tracked 332 AI releases this week. 85% were noise.
summary: An AI news tracker ingesting 332 items weekly filters out 85% as undifferentiated noise—repackaged product updates, incomplete repositories, incremental research variations, and fine-tuned model rebrands. Of the remaining 130 items worth developer attention, only about 15 represent genuinely important developments that should change how developers think about building. The challenge lies in classification calibration as the distribution of what constitutes 'real news' shifts constantly in the fast-moving AI field.
content:
332 items. That's how many AI releases, papers, repos, and tool launches we ingested last week into the tracker I've been building.
I sat down this morning to review the weekly summary. After 6 months of running this, I have a number burned into my brain: 85%.
That's the percentage of ingest that gets filtered as noise before anything reaches a reader.
Not fake. Not spam. Just... undifferentiated. Here's the breakdown from this week's batch:
47 "we're excited to announce" blog posts — repackaged product updates with zero technical content
61 GitHub repos with a README that says "coming soon" or "WIP"
29 papers that are incremental variations on RLHF fine-tuning with no usable results section
38 "model launches" that are fine-tunes of Llama or Mistral with different system prompts and a new brand name
27 ecosystem announcements (funding rounds, team changes, partnerships) — real news, but not useful if you're building
That's 202 items out of 332. Gone.
What's left: 130 items worth a developer's attention. Of those, maybe 15 are genuinely important — things that change what you should be building or how you should think about the stack.
I didn't expect this ratio when I started. I thought AI news was signal-dense. The opposite is true. The field moves fast, but most of the "movement" is noise masquerading as signal.
The hard part isn't ingestion. It's classification. We've tried three approaches:
Rule-based filtering — keyword blacklists, source trust scores. Works for obvious noise. Misses subtle stuff.
LLM scoring — prompt asking "is this novel?" Gets fooled by confident-sounding fluff.
Hybrid + human audit — where we are now. An LLM pre-filters; I spot-check 20-30 items per day for calibration.
The calibration loop is the thing nobody writes about. Every week the distribution shifts slightly. A term that meant "genuine model architecture" last month now means "branding wrapper."
After 6 months: the 15% that matters hasn't actually changed.
The absolute count of rea

zh.title: 我这周追踪了332个AI发布，85%都是噪音。
zh.summary: 一个AI新闻追踪器每周摄入332个项目，将其中85%过滤为无差别的噪音——重新包装的产品更新、不完整的代码仓库、增量研究变体和微调后的模型品牌重塑。在剩余的130个值得开发者关注的项目中，只有约15个代表真正重要的发展，应该改变开发者对构建方式的思考方式。挑战在于分类校准，因为在这个快速发展的AI领域，什么构成"真正新闻"的分布在不断变化。

title: I built a 11-language B2B site on Astro 6 + Cloudflare Workers — here's what surprised me
summary: A developer built an 11-language B2B site on Astro 6 and Cloudflare Workers with zero monthly infrastructure costs, supporting dynamic edge-generated OG images, full-text search, and email-based lead capture entirely within free tiers. The most significant discovery was Astro 6's integration with Cloudflare's workerd runtime, eliminating the classic problem where code works locally but breaks in production due to runtime differences. Using Content Layer API for 250+ MDX files provided a simple unified structure for managing multilingual content across guides, blog posts, case studies, and services.

zh.title: 我用Astro 6 + Cloudflare Workers构建了一个11语言B2B网站——以下是出乎我意料的事情
zh.summary: 一位开发者使用Astro 6和Cloudflare Workers构建了一个11语言的B2B网站，基础设施成本为零，支持动态边缘生成的OG图像、全文搜索和基于电子邮件的线索捕获，全部在免费额度内实现。最重要的发现是Astro 6与Cloudflare workerd运行时的集成，解决了代码在本地工作但在生产环境中因运行时差异而失效的经典问题。使用Content Layer API管理250多个MDX文件，为跨指南、博客文章、案例研究和服务的多语言内容管理提供了简洁统一的结构。

title: Your chatbot might be saying things you never intended
summary: Most chatbot failures stem from implementation and prompting rather than the underlying model, including prompt injection, off-script responses, and sensitive data exposure. PromptBrake offers security testing to catch these issues before launch by simulating realistic customer conversations. Comprehensive chatbot testing before release is critical as many teams ship AI chatbots without adequate pre-launch testing under pressure.
content:
AI chatbots are getting shipped fast — but many teams still don’t test how they behave under pressure before launch.
We’ve been building chatbot security tests at PromptBrake to help catch things like:
prompt injection
off-script responses
risky promises
broken escalation flows
sensitive data exposure
The interesting part is that most failures don’t come from the model itself — they come from how the chatbot is wired, prompted, and exposed through the app.
I recorded a short walkthrough showing how we test a chatbot API using realistic customer conversations before release.
Would love feedback from others building AI products or customer-facing chatbots.
Demo video: [https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=CsJdVmX3dhc]

zh.title: 你的聊天机器人可能在说你从未打算说的话
zh.summary: 大多数聊天机器人故障源于实现和提示词而不是底层模型，包括提示词注入、超出预期的回应和敏感数据泄露。PromptBrake提供安全测试来通过模拟真实的客户对话在发布前捕获这些问题。在发布前进行全面的聊天机器人测试至关重要，因为许多团队在时间压力下发布AI聊天机器人但没有进行足够的发布前测试。

title: Cx Dev Log — 2026-05-02
summary: The Cx compiler project merged 24 PRs documenting the intermediate representation strategy for unary operations, which encodes negate and boolean-not as two-operand forms to minimize backend work. The documentation clarifies architectural decisions that enable uniform instruction patterns for both arithmetic and comparison tasks. The merge work also updated development logs and roadmap documentation spanning from March 29 to May 1, 2026.
content:
Clearing a backlog of stale PRs might sound like mundane maintenance, but today's work on Cx has brought a key piece of technical documentation into focus. It's all about the unary lowering strategy in the Intermediate Representation (IR), which isn't visible without those extra insights.
Ever noticed Cx's IR doesn't handle negate or boolean-not instructions directly? Instead, we've decided to encode them in a two-operand form:
Op::Minus becomes 0 - value


Op::Not becomes value == 0



This choice minimizes the work for backends, needing just one instruction pattern for both arithmetic and comparison tasks. The nuances of this strategy are now documented in src/ir/lower.rs, thanks to a recent commit (2d665a4) on stokowski/cx-6-document-unary-lowering. These 25 lines of comments don't alter code behavior but offer crucial context to understand our IR encoding decisions.
The documentation sits on a branch yet to reach main, inheriting a chunk of history from Phases 10-11 IR backend work. Although it won't impact the system since it's purely comments, this documentation is central to understanding our IR processing.
With PRs #29 to #52 now merged, the daily logs from the past month are no longer sitting in limbo. These merges connect entries from 2026-03-29 to 2026-05-01 and update the docs/frontend/ROADMAP.md. While this task doesn't shift Cx's functionality forward, it restores traceability and organization, areas highlighted in prior logs as growing liabilities.
Maintaining a clear record from late March onwards, even without language revisions, reinforces our historical and developmental context. It's all about keeping the narrative straight, especially when planning future pathways.
The real challenge lies with submain's drift. Holding 22 unmerged commits, submain is where version 5.0 resides with 117 tests done and all blockers fixed, while main lags at version 4.9 with just 78 tests. New branches are developing from submain, not main, emphasizing the difference

zh.title: Cx 开发日志 — 2026-05-02
zh.summary: Cx 编译器项目合并了 24 个 PR，记录了一元操作的中间表示策略，将取反和布尔非编码为双操作数形式以最小化后端工作。文档澄清了架构决策，使算术和比较任务能够使用统一的指令模式。合并工作还更新了 2026 年 3 月 29 日至 5 月 1 日期间的开发日志和路线图文档。

title: Why I built Parlotype: a privacy-first voice-to-English desktop app on .NET 10
summary: Parlotype is a Windows desktop application built on .NET 10 that translates speech from a user's native language to English and inserts the text directly into active applications without cloud connectivity. The tool addresses friction for non-native English speakers who frequently need translation for emails, code comments, and documentation by combining Windows 11 dictation with on-device speech recognition and translation. The developer prioritized cross-platform design using Avalonia UI and Whisper.net for future macOS and Linux support despite current Windows-only release.
content:
The friction


I've been shipping production code for 20 years across five languages — C, C++, Java, Scala, and now C#. My English is decent enough for daily work, but it's not native.
So whenever I want a sharper adjective in an email, or a phrase that doesn't read as translated, I still reach for Google Translate. Sometimes I dictate into it. Sometimes I type — which is slower. And if I'm on a machine without a Russian keyboard layout, the friction goes up another notch.
Multiple times a day. Across email, MS Teams, PR descriptions, design docs.
I finally got tired of switching context, and built a tool to skip it.
Windows 11 has perfectly fine built-in dictation. But it doesn't translate — and translation is the half that matters for non-native English speakers like me.
The workflow I needed:
Press a global hotkey
Speak in my native language
Get English text inserted directly into whatever app I'm in
No browser tab. No copy-paste. Nothing sent to the cloud.
That's Parlotype.
  
  



  
  
  The stack


The first version is Windows-only, but I picked every piece with cross-platform support in mind from day one:
.NET 10 — runtime
Avalonia UI 12 — cross-platform desktop UI (tray-based)
Whisper.net — on-device speech recognition (OpenAI Whisper bindings for .NET)
Silero VAD — voice activity detection (ONNX-based)
NAudio — Windows audio capture (WASAPI)
CommunityToolkit.Mvvm — MVVM source generators
SharpHook — cross-platform global hotkeys
A few decisions worth highlighting:
Avalonia over MAUI. I needed a real desktop tray app on Windows/Linux/macOS. MAUI's desktop story is still uneven; Avalonia handles tray, hotkeys, and native window chrome cleanly across all three platforms.
Whisper.net over Whisper.cpp directly. Whisper.cpp is the reference implementation, but Whisper.net wraps it with idiomatic C# APIs and managed memory handling — meaningful when integrating with the rest of a .NET app.
Silero VAD over WebRTC VAD. WebRTC's VAD is older and noisier on modern a

zh.title: 我为什么开发 Parlotype：一个基于 .NET 10 的隐私优先语音转英文桌面应用
zh.summary: Parlotype 是一个基于 .NET 10 构建的 Windows 桌面应用程序，可将用户的母语语音翻译成英文，并直接将文本插入到活动应用程序中，无需云连接。该工具通过结合 Windows 11 听写、设备端语音识别和翻译功能，为频繁需要为电子邮件、代码注释和文档进行翻译的非母语英语使用者解决了问题。开发者优先考虑使用 Avalonia UI 和 Whisper.net 的跨平台设计，以支持未来的 macOS 和 Linux 版本，尽管目前仅限 Windows 发布。

title: server-side sharded watch is Kubernetes admitting the control plane has a data-scale problem
summary: Kubernetes v1.36's server-side sharded list and watch feature reveals that the control plane has fundamentally become a data distribution system, where the bottleneck shifts from storage to feeding continuous updates to numerous watchers. Modern Kubernetes clusters host not just workloads but dozens of controllers, autoscalers, policy engines, and observability systems all continuously watching resources, making the challenge of coordinating change awareness more critical than storing objects. This architectural evolution exposes data-scale problems in Kubernetes' watch model at serious scale.
content:
Kubernetes scalability conversations usually start with the obvious stuff.
How many nodes? How many pods? How large is the cluster? How much etcd pain can one organization spiritually endure before someone says “maybe we should split this thing” in a meeting and everyone pretends they were already thinking it?
Fair questions.
But I think Kubernetes v1.36 server-side sharded list and watch points at a quieter, more interesting problem:
the control plane is becoming a data distribution system, and the hard part is no longer only storing objects. It is feeding every client that wants to continuously know what changed.
That sounds boring. Good. Boring infrastructure primitives are where the real architecture leaks out.

One of the reasons Kubernetes feels so powerful is the watch model.
Controllers do not constantly ask, “hey, did anything happen?” like an anxious intern refreshing a dashboard. They list the current state, then watch for changes. The API server becomes the coordination point for a lot of little loops trying to move reality toward desired state.
That model is elegant.
It is also everywhere.
Your deployment controller watches Deployments and ReplicaSets. Your autoscaler watches workloads and metrics-adjacent signals. Your policy engine watches resources. Your GitOps controller watches cluster state. Your service mesh watches endpoints and config. Your observability stack watches things. Your custom controllers watch things. Your shiny AI platform controller, written during a suspiciously optimistic sprint, also watches things.
Eventually the cluster has fewer “users” than “watchers.”
And that is the part people undercount.
A mature Kubernetes environment is not just a pile of workloads. It is a pile of clients trying to keep a local mental model of the cluster.
We still call it the Kubernetes API server, which is technically correct, but incomplete.
At small scale, it feels like an API. You send requests. You get responses. Nice.
At serious scale, it beha

zh.title: 服务器端分片监视是 Kubernetes 承认控制平面存在数据规模问题
zh.summary: Kubernetes v1.36 的服务器端分片列表和监视功能揭示了控制平面从根本上已成为数据分发系统，其中瓶颈从存储转向向众多监视器提供持续更新。现代 Kubernetes 集群不仅托管工作负载，还托管数十个控制器、自动扩展器、策略引擎和可观察性系统，所有这些都在不断监视资源，使协调变化感知的挑战比存储对象更加关键。这种架构演进暴露了 Kubernetes 监视模型在严重规模下的数据规模问题。
